name: Mani
description: Mani, in Norse mythology, is the personification of the Moon. He is the son of Mundilfari and brother of Sól, the Sun. Mani is described as being incredibly handsome and captivating. According to the Prose Edda, the children are so beautiful that Mundilfari names them Moon (Mani) and Sun (Sól), which angers the gods. As punishment for Mundilfari's arrogance, the gods place Sól and Mani in the sky to drive the Sun and Moon chariots, respectively. Mani's chariot is pursued by the wolf Hati Hróðvitnisson, who seeks to devour him. The phases of the moon are explained as the result of Hati getting closer or further away from Mani. Mani is also said to have taken two children, Bil and Hjúki, from Earth as they were drawing water from a well. They now accompany him on his journey across the sky, serving as his attendants. Mani's role is vital in the Norse cosmos, maintaining the celestial balance and the passage of time. His eventual fate, like that of Sól, is to be consumed by the wolves at Ragnarök, the end of the world. He represents the lunar cycle, the ebb and flow, and the beauty of the night sky. He possesses the power to control the tides and influence the growth of plants, as well as being associated with the passage of time and the prediction of the future based on lunar cycles. Mani's influence is subtle but pervasive, a constant presence in the night sky guiding travelers and shaping the world.
subtitle: The Norse God of the Moon

keyMyths.title: The Naming of Mani and Sól
keyMyths.description: Mundilfari named his children Moon and Sun, angering the gods who forced them to drive the celestial chariots as punishment. This myth explains the origin and roles of Mani and Sól in the Norse cosmos.

keyMyths.title: Mani and the Children Bil and Hjúki
keyMyths.description: Mani abducted the children Bil and Hjúki as they were drawing water, taking them to the sky to serve as his attendants. This myth explains the presence of these two figures alongside Mani in the night sky.

keyMyths.title: The Pursuit of Mani by Hati
keyMyths.description: The wolf Hati Hróðvitnisson relentlessly pursues Mani across the sky, seeking to devour him. The phases of the moon are explained by Hati's varying proximity to Mani during his celestial journey.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson

cultural.modernLegacy: Mani's influence is subtle but present in modern Neopaganism and Heathenry, where the lunar cycle and its connection to Mani are observed. He appears in literature and art, often symbolizing the moon's beauty and mystery.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Mani's parentage is explicitly stated in Norse texts: he is the son of Mundilfari. The name Mundilfari translates to 'the one moving according to appointed times,' suggesting a connection to celestial movements. His sister, Sól, is the personification of the Sun. The siblings' beauty was so striking that Mundilfari named them Moon and Sun, an act the gods deemed arrogant. As punishment, the gods placed Mani and Sól in the heavens, tasking them with driving the moon and sun across the sky in chariots. This cosmic arrangement established the cycle of day and night, governed by the deities' perpetual journey. Mani's origins thus lie in the personification of the moon and the cosmological order of the Norse world.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Mani's most prominent role is his ceaseless journey across the sky, driving the moon chariot. He is perpetually pursued by the wolf Hati Hróðvitnisson, who seeks to devour him, mirroring the Norse explanation for lunar eclipses. According to the Prose Edda, Mani controls the waxing and waning of the moon, showcasing his influence over lunar phases. He is also described as taking two children, Bil and Hjúki, from Earth while they were fetching water, and they accompany him on his celestial journey. These children represent the phases of the moon. The pursuit by Hati will continue until Ragnarök, when the wolf finally catches Mani and devours him, plunging the world into darkness, which marks the end of the current cosmic order.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Direct archaeological evidence of a specific cult dedicated solely to Mani is limited. However, the moon held significance in broader Norse pagan practices. The lunar cycle influenced agricultural activities and seasonal celebrations. While no specific rituals are explicitly linked to Mani, lunar phases were likely considered auspicious or inauspicious times for certain activities. The lack of direct evidence does not negate the importance of the moon in the Norse worldview. It's likely that reverence for Mani was intertwined with general astronomical observations and the cyclical nature of time, and incorporated into broader rituals honoring the cosmos and the natural order.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Mani's legacy persists primarily through the understanding of Norse mythology and its impact on modern culture. He is a symbol of the moon and its cyclical nature. He appears in modern fiction, art, and music inspired by Norse mythology. In Neopagan and Heathen communities, Mani represents the lunar principle and is sometimes invoked in rituals related to lunar energies or cycles. The study of Mani provides insight into the Norse understanding of astronomy and their personification of natural phenomena. While not a major figure in terms of surviving textual references, Mani remains a vital component of the Norse cosmological framework and continues to inspire artistic and spiritual interpretations.

shortDescription: Mani is the Norse god of the Moon, tasked with driving the moon across the sky in his chariot, eternally pursued by a wolf. Brother of Sól (the Sun).
